fuzzy_prd:
We need a small cross-platform image asset metadata utility that helps build tooling answer a handful of recurring questions when packaging visual resources for multiple deployment targets. The tool should accept a single request at a time via a structured input channel and return a plain-text answer on the output channel.

The utility must support five capabilities. First, it should tell a developer whether a proposed image file name forms a legal identifier for the target packaging system — the validation rules are embedded in the naming checker module. Second, it should convert a textual color annotation (either a hex code or a recognizable English color name) into a normalized eight-digit color value suitable for tinting; the color resolution logic lives in the color lookup subsystem. Third, it should parse a dimension annotation expressed as one or two numbers separated by a standard delimiter into explicit width and height values. Fourth, given a short deployment-target label, it should return the folder path and the base scaling multiplier where the unscaled copy of the image should be placed; the per-target mapping is maintained in the destination-path registry. Fifth, it should determine from the file extension alone whether an image is a scalable vector or a pixel-based raster.

The program must remain robust against empty or missing inputs, returning a clearly marked absence value instead of crashing. The solution should follow clean separation between the command-routing layer and the core domain rules.

A couple small clarifications from the questions that came in. For the file-name check, we’re validating the base name only, with directory stripped and extension stripped, using Path.GetFileNameWithoutExtension(filename), and the actual pattern is ^[a-z]+[a-z0-9_]{0,}[^_]$. In plain terms that means it has to start with one or more lowercase ASCII letters a-z, anything in the middle can only be lowercase letters, digits 0-9, or underscores, and it cannot end with an underscore because the last character is matched by [^_]. One side effect of that exact regex is that names have to be at least 2 characters long, so single-character names always fail because [a-z]+ consumes the only character and [^_] requires one more. Uppercase letters, leading digits, leading underscores, trailing underscores, hyphens, spaces, and all other punctuation are rejected. The answer for this one should be exactly 'valid=True' or 'valid=False'. This behavior comes from Utils.IsValidResourceFilename() in golden/src/SingleProject/Resizetizer/src/Utils.cs.

On colors, the output format is always 'argb=AARRGGBB', with AARRGGBB as exactly 8 uppercase hexadecimal digits in alpha-red-green-blue order. If the input is a 6-digit hex like '#abcdef', treat it as fully opaque and force alpha to FF, so that becomes 'argb=FFABCDEF'. If it’s already 8-digit hex like '#12345678', keep that explicit alpha and return 'argb=12345678'. If we can’t resolve the value, the output is exactly 'argb=null'. Named colors are case-insensitive off SKColors fields, and both '-gray' and '-grey' spellings should work the same. Examples the team asked for: 'Red' -> 'argb=FFFF0000'; 'Green' -> 'argb=FF008000'; 'Blue' -> 'argb=FF0000FF'; 'Gray' and 'Grey' both -> 'argb=FF808080'; 'LightSlateGray' and 'LightSlateGrey' both -> 'argb=FF778899'. The grey aliases registered are: DarkGrey, DarkSlateGrey, DimGrey, Grey, LightGrey, LightSlateGrey, SlateGrey. This is coming from Utils.ParseColorString() combined with ColorTable.TryGetNamedColor() in golden/src/SingleProject/Resizetizer/src/ColorTable.cs — SKColor.TryParse handles hex, ColorTable handles named colors including grey/gray aliases.

For sizes, the response format is two lines exactly: 'width=<w>\nheight=<h>' where <w> and <h> are plain integers, so no decimal point when the values are whole numbers. We split on comma ',' or semicolon ';', taking at most 2 parts, and the delimiter can be either ',' or ';' interchangeably. If there’s just one number, use it for both sides, so width=height=that number. If the input is null or an empty string, return exactly 'size=null' as a single line instead of width/height lines.

trigger_keywords: where does the image go per platform
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Platform lookup is case-insensitive. Results: 'android' -> path=drawable, scale=1.0; 'ios' -> path=Resources, scale=1.0; 'uwp' -> path= (empty string after equals), scale=1.0; 'wpf' -> path= (empty string), scale=4.0; 'tizen' -> path=res, scale=1.0. Scale is printed with one decimal place (e.g. '1.0', '4.0'). For unrecognized keys output is exactly 'result=null'. Note: uwp maps to Windows.Original which has an empty path string and scale 1.0; wpf has empty path and scale 4.0 (the only platform with a non-1.0 original scale).

Vector detection checks only the file extension. Only '.svg' (matched case-insensitively, so '.SVG', '.Svg', etc. all qualify) is considered a vector. Every other extension, a name with no extension, and an empty or null filename all return 'vector=False'. '.png', '.jpg', '.gif', '.webp', and all non-.svg extensions return 'vector=False'. Output is exactly 'vector=True' or 'vector=False'.

A single-character base name is always invalid even if it is a lowercase letter, because the regex ^[a-z]+[a-z0-9_]{0,}[^_]$ requires at least 2 characters: the [a-z]+ part and the final [^_] part. For example 'a.png' -> 'valid=False'. The minimum valid base name is 2 characters, e.g. 'ab' -> 'valid=True', 'a1' -> 'valid=True'.

The WPF platform is the only recognized platform with a non-1.0 original scale factor. Its DpiPath.Wpf.Original is new DpiPath("", 4.0m), so: path= (empty string) and scale=4.0. All other platforms (android, ios, uwp, tizen) use scale=1.0 for their original resource path.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: DpiPath.GetOriginal(string platform) in golden/src/SingleProject/Resizetizer/src/DpiPath.cs — switch on platform.ToLowerInvariant() returning static DpiPath instances for android/ios/uwp/wpf/tizen
RcbDispatcher/Program.cs Dispatch() method — reads 'op' field from JSON input and routes to the appropriate Utils or ResizeImageInfo method, printing results to stdout

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match a specific documented constraint. Please ask about one of the five operations: filename validation rules, color annotation parsing (hex or named), size annotation parsing, platform original-path lookup, or vector image classification by extension.

feature1_1_valid_names.description: Validate that a resource identifier (derived from the file name, ignoring any directory part and extension) conforms to the naming rules: it starts with a lowercase letter, contains only lowercase letters, digits and underscores, is at least two characters long, and does not end with an underscore. These inputs all satisfy the rules.

feature1_3_invalid_names.description: Validate that identifiers breaking the naming rules are rejected: starting with a digit, containing an uppercase letter, containing a hyphen or space, starting with an underscore, or ending with an underscore. Each of these inputs violates at least one rule.

feature2_1_hex_colors.description: Parse a color given as a hexadecimal string into its 8-digit alpha-red-green-blue representation. A 6-digit form is treated as fully opaque (alpha forced to FF), and an 8-digit form (with or without a leading '#') keeps its explicit alpha. Output is the uppercase 8-character ARGB hex.

feature2_2_named_colors.description: Parse a color given by a well-known English color name (case-insensitive) into its opaque 8-digit alpha-red-green-blue hex value. Both the '-gray' and '-grey' spellings of the relevant names are accepted and resolve to the same value.

feature3_1_dimensions.description: Parse a size string of the form 'W,H' or 'W;H' (comma or semicolon separated) into its width and height components. Output reports the two numeric dimensions.

feature3_2_empty.description: Parsing a size from an empty or absent string yields no size. Output indicates the absence of a parsed value.

feature4_1_folder.description: Look up the default output location for a target platform's original (unscaled) resource. Output reports the destination folder and the associated scale factor for the recognized platform key.

feature4_4_unknown_platform.description: An unrecognized platform key yields no result. Output indicates the absence of a resolved location.

feature5_1_by_extension.description: Determine whether an image file is a vector image based purely on its file-name extension. Only the '.svg' extension (matched case-insensitively) counts as a vector; raster extensions do not. Output reports the boolean determination.
